caveats: a: Occupation mix is four-digit, not 336212.
a: Exposure is a task mapping rather than observed labor removal.

rho: Safety requirements preserve accountable engineering and inspection.

e: Enterprise ownership cannot be inferred from establishment coding alone.

s5: Private small-company transactions are incompletely reported.

q: Retention differs greatly between commodity dry vans and specialized tank or lowboy trailers.
d5: Industrial-production revisions can be large.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Product revenue from repeat fleet orders and dealer sales of standard or configured dry van, refrigerated, flatbed, dump, tank, specialty, and chassis equipment, with options and parts as ancillary revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Utility and light-truck trailers, travel trailers, captive plants, repair and rental businesses, distributors without manufacturing, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly manufacturing truck trailers, semitrailers, truck-trailer chassis, cargo-container chassis, detachable trailer bodies, and detachable chassis for external fleets, dealers, leasing companies, and logistics customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader NAICS 336200 occupation mix and not AI exposure. I assign partial exposure to quoting, order configuration, engineering-document reuse, procurement, planning, compliance, quality records, and customer service, while treating most assembly and fabrication as physically constrained. Because trailers may be more standardized than RV and body products in the broader group, reuse opportunities could make the proxy understate exposure, while plant-heavy small firms could make it overstate exposure.
caveats: Occupation mix is four-digit, not 336212.
Exposure is a task mapping rather than observed labor removal.
a.change_evidence: Six-digit wage-weighted SOC employment and task-time observations from independent trailer plants, especially quote-to-order, engineering changes, scheduling, purchasing, quality, warranty, and customer support.
a.rationale: Production occupations are 65.21% of employment in the broader group and assemblers/fabricators alone are 31.75%, limiting direct AI substitution. Information work around repeat designs and high-volume orders remains material.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial production directly measures real US 336212 output, while BTS freight forecasts measure the downstream activity that uses trailers. Neither measures future constant-quality demand specifically for LMM firms. Because current output is unusually depressed, mean reversion can make a five-year current ratio exceed the underlying freight-growth rate; failure to recover would make that adjustment overstate demand.
caveats: Industrial-production revisions can be large.
Freight growth does not map one-for-one to trailer purchases.
A depressed starting point makes the ratio especially cycle-sensitive.
d5.change_evidence: Current ACT Research or FTR trailer orders, backlog-to-build ratios, cancellations, fleet age, lease utilization, replacement budgets, and segment production forecasts through 2031.
d5.rationale: Annual industrial production fell from 98.55 in 2022 to 55.65 in 2025 and was 54.83 seasonally adjusted in May 2026, indicating a severe cycle trough. BTS expects freight tonnage to grow 50% from 2020 to 2050, with trucks retaining the predominant role. The range allows continued fleet caution, partial normalization, or a strong replacement rebound.

caveats: The provided n is margin-bridged rather than observed.
Enterprise ownership cannot be inferred from establishment coding alone.
e.change_evidence: A current firm census mapped from 336212 establishments to ultimate owners, with normalized EBITDA, external-manufacturing revenue, fleet repeat rate, customer concentration, and independence flags.
e.rationale: Regional specialty manufacturers and private trailer brands can fit the EBITDA band and repeatedly serve external fleets. Eligibility is reduced by large consolidated producers, captive units, firms below the band, commodity job shops, and businesses whose revenue is distribution or repair rather than manufacturing.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 336212 ($2,575,042,384) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 336212 ($17,146,305,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $390,550 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336212); class <5: 110 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,592,218 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $143,937 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 68 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,337,059 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $211,270 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,526,632 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $318,807 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 157 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,383,561 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,209,874 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $61,165,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,529,376 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $373,157,688 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $33,733,455 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 199

caveats: Consolidation and imports can displace lens firms even if the operator requirement persists.
o.change_evidence: Evidence that fleets broadly self-manufacture trailers or that automated contract plants eliminate rather than transform independent manufacturers across major trailer categories.
o.rationale: A trailer remains a physical, regulated load-carrying asset that must be designed, fabricated, inspected, certified, warranted, and delivered by an accountable manufacturer. AI can change labor content but cannot replace that operator within five years.

caveats: PPI observes industry selling prices, not AI-benefit retention.
Retention differs greatly between commodity dry vans and specialized tank or lowboy trailers.
q.change_evidence: Fleet and dealer contracts showing rebid cadence, volume tiers, material escalation, open-book costing, productivity clauses, and margin bridges following documented automation.
q.rationale: Manufacturers can retain benefits through faster quoting, mix, throughput, and lead-time performance, especially in specialty models. Large fleets, leasing companies, dealers, competitive bids, standardized specifications, and visible producer prices transmit a substantial share through lower prices or slower increases.

caveats: No trailer-industry study directly observes five-year implementation.
Safety requirements preserve accountable engineering and inspection.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-plant case evidence of sustained AI workflow adoption with realized labor hours, cycle time, quality escapes, rework, and integration cost over at least 24 months.
rho.rationale: Standard configurations and repeated workflows allow implementation in commercial and planning functions within five years. Legacy ERP/MES systems, weak data, dealer customization, safety testing, plant integration, and change management prevent full realization.

s5.bridge: Census ABS owner-age tables cover employer businesses broadly, not trailer manufacturers or completed control transfers. I use owner aging as succession pressure and reduce for internal succession, closures, minority recapitalizations, corporate ownership, and failed processes; cyclicality may delay transactions, biasing an age-only proxy upward.
caveats: Owner age is not a direct deal-flow measure.
Private small-company transactions are incompletely reported.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of verified PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and industry-association deals for US trailer manufacturers, tagged by control status and divided by an eligible-firm denominator.
s5.rationale: A meaningful independent manufacturer base creates succession and consolidation opportunities, but current production weakness, capital needs, customer concentration, and environmental or product-liability diligence reduce executable transfers.

principal_driver: A depressed replacement cycle plus durable truck-freight use creates recovery upside around a necessary physical asset.
principal_weakness: Production labor is overwhelmingly physical and demand is exceptionally cyclical at the current measurement date.
